articles.title: Goa police file second charge sheet in Birch by Romeo Lane nightclub fire case | India News - Hindustan Times
articles.full_text:
Goa police file second charge sheet in Birch by Romeo Lane nightclub fire case
Goa Police filed a 2,306-page chargesheet in the Birch by Romeo Lane fire case, alleging forged documents and safety violations that led to 25 deaths.
The Goa police on Thursday filed a chargesheet against nightclub owners Gaurav and Saurabh Luthra in a second FIR linked to the December 2025 fire at Birch by Romeo Lane in Goa’s Arpora village that killed 25 people.
The 2,306-page chargesheet names 50 witnesses and five accused, including the Luthra brothers; their partner in M/s Being GS Hospitality Goa Arpora LLP, which owned the Birch by Romeo Lane nightclub, Ajay Gupta; Dinesh Khera; and excise department clerk Gauresh Gawas.
According to the chargesheet, the Luthra brothers and Gupta submitted a forged no-objection certificate from the local health centre while applying for an excise licence, which was later issued to them.
An NOC from the health department is necessary for a club to obtain permission to operate.
The Arpora fire tragedy at Birch by Romeo Lane, an illegally operating nightclub, occurred on the intervening night of December 6 and 7, 2025, killing 25 people — five tourists and 20 staff members. The brothers, who had flown to Thailand within hours of the tragedy, were repatriated to India after an Interpol notice was issued against them and their passports were suspended.
The FIR, filed on December 7, charges them under Sections 105 (culpable homicide not amounting to murder), 125(a) and (b) (endangering life and personal safety), and 287 (negligent conduct with fire or combustible matter) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
A second FIR alleging cheating and forgery was also filed against the Luthras and Ajay Gupta for allegedly fabricating documents as part of the application process.
The chargesheet said that due to the irresponsible acts of the accused, 25 people lost their lives, causing “irreparable loss which shattered 25 families, reflecting gross criminal negligence and utter disregard for human life and complete failure of the accused to adhere to statutory and safety norms.”

articles.title: Dairy Drink Sales Surge Over 50% As Heatwave Boosts Demand - Whalesbook
articles.full_text:
India's dairy beverage sector is witnessing double-digit growth this summer as consumers switch to healthier, packaged alternatives like lassi and buttermilk. While demand is high, the sector faces ongoing pressure from rising raw milk procurement costs, forcing organized players to focus on value-added products to protect profit margins.
What Happened
India’s packaged dairy beverage market is seeing a massive uptick in demand this summer. Driven by record-breaking temperatures and a growing consumer preference for health-focused alternatives, major brands are reporting double-digit growth in categories like lassi, buttermilk, and flavored milk. Industry data shows that some segments, including protein-based drinks and specific buttermilk variants, have surged by over 50% to 100% in recent months. This shift highlights a broader move among Indian consumers away from sugary carbonated soft drinks toward products perceived as more nutritious and hydrating.
Why This Matters For Investors
For investors, this demand surge is a double-edged sword. While it drives revenue growth for dairy companies, the profitability of the sector remains sensitive to raw milk procurement costs. Dairy processors are currently dealing with a phase where they must balance strong market demand with the rising cost of raw milk, which has faced inflationary pressure in the first half of 2026. Companies that successfully shift their product mix toward value-added products—such as cheese, whey protein, and specialized dairy beverages—are generally better positioned to maintain healthy operating margins compared to those reliant solely on liquid milk sales, where pricing power is often constrained by local competition and consumer sensitivity.
The Margin And Input Cost Test
Although sales volumes are rising, the industry is entering a period of “margin recalibration.” Raw milk prices have seen upward pressure, with industry reports indicating projected increases of ₹3–4 per litre in certain regions due to feed shortages and supply chain constraints. For listed companies like Parag Milk Foods, Hatsun Agro, Heritage Foods, and Dodla Dairy, the key investor monitorable is their ability to pass these rising input costs to consumers through phased price hikes. Investors should be aware that while top-line revenue growth for the organized dairy sector is projected at 13–15% for the current fiscal year, operating margins may remain under pressure as firms absorb some of these costs to keep their brands competitive against unorganized and local players.
The Shift Toward Value-Added Products
Companies are increasingly focusing on the "premiumization" of dairy. The success of brands like Parag Milk Foods' Avvatar (whey protein) and Gowardhan (ghee/cheese) illustrates this trend. By expanding into high-margin categories, these companies aim to reduce their dependence on the low-margin liquid milk business. This strategy is essential because liquid milk is highly sensitive to price changes and logistics costs, whereas value-added dairy products often enjoy better brand loyalty and pricing flexibility. The growth in the quick-commerce segment has further accelerated this, as it allows companies to reach urban consumers faster, potentially lowering inventory hold times for perishable beverages.
What Investors Should Track Next
Investors monitoring the dairy space should focus on a few key triggers in the coming quarters. First, watch for management commentary on raw milk procurement prices, as sustained inflation here could threaten profitability. Second, track the volume growth of value-added products (like cheese, paneer, and protein drinks) versus the growth in mass-market milk. Finally, keep an eye on how companies manage their marketing and distribution spends, especially as they compete for shelf space in the rapidly expanding quick-commerce and modern retail channels. Persistent cost pressure without sufficient price hikes could lead to weaker-than-expected earnings reports in the upcoming quarterly results.
